pathophysiology_summary: Tinea pedis is dermatophyte infection of the feet, favouring the warm, moist interdigital spaces (scaling, soggy white maceration, fissuring, itch/burning) and sometimes a dry hyperkeratotic 'moccasin' pattern on the soles; it is acquired from communal wet areas/footwear and thrives in occlusion and sweat. It is benign but the macerated, fissured web spaces are a common portal for secondary bacterial infection. The ground-truth task is to distinguish it from contact dermatitis and erythrasma, and — crucially — to screen for secondary bacterial cellulitis (confluent hot spreading tender erythema, red streaking/lymphangitis, fever) and diabetic-foot infection, which change management to antibiotics ± urgent care. Management is a topical antifungal, moisture control (dry between the toes, antiseptic soaks for macerated skin, cotton socks, footwear rotation) and treating any secondary infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Interdigital scaling/maceration + moccasin soles + gym-shower exposure.
Fissures are entry points — screen confluent spreading hot erythema, streaking, fever.
Interdigital maceration with gym-shower exposure favours tinea; erythrasma fluoresces coral-red on Wood's lamp.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Tinea pedis leads; secondary cellulitis and diabetic-foot infection must be screened before conservative management.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic interdigital/moccasin tinea, no cellulitis/streaking/fever, not diabetic — topical-treatable tinea pedis.
No spreading erythema/streaking/fever; escalation target if it develops (fissures are the portal).

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative cellulitis/diabetes screen, tinea pedis leads; topical antifungal + moisture control, with a cellulitis safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; clears with topical antifungal + moisture control over ~2–4 weeks.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is topical-treatable tinea pedis.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial cellulitis / diabetic-foot infection — confluent hot spreading tender redness up the foot/leg, red streaking (lymphangitis), fever, or infection in a diabetic/immunocompromised or ischaemic foot
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cellulitis needs antibiotics ± ED; diabetic-foot infection can progress rapidly.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ascending cellulitis / sepsis / limb-threatening diabetic-foot infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening for spreading erythema/streaking/fever arising from the fissured web spaces.
Powder clumps in fissures and irritates; antiseptic drying and antifungal are appropriate.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for deep infection / pulses / neuropathy (diabetic foot)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical antifungal + antiseptic soaks + moisture/footwear control; GP/ED if cellulitis develops (especially in a diabetic foot).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing secondary cellulitis or a diabetic-foot infection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Topical antifungal (terbinafine/azole) for tinea pedis, continued after clearing; keep the feet dry (dry between toes, cotton socks, footwear rotation); antiseptic soaks for macerated/fissured skin. Treat secondary bacterial infection with antibiotics; have a lower threshold in diabetics.
